prohibited.label: Prohibit all
prohibited.description: Prohibition of any use of AI, with no exceptions.

prohibited_except_assisted_research.label: Prohibit except for Westlaw, Lexis, etc.
prohibited_except_assisted_research.description: The order/rule prohibits litigants from using AI with an exception for legal tools such as Westlaw and Lexis.

disclosure_with_traditional_verification.label: Permit with verification via traditional resources
disclosure_with_traditional_verification.description: The order/rule permits litigants to use AI as long as they verify the accuracy of any AI-generated content using "print reporters" or "traditional legal databases" (or "other reliable means" or similar language).

disclosure_required.label: Permit with disclosure/certification
disclosure_required.description: The order/rule permits litigants to use any AI, but litigants must disclose their AI usage and certify that they have reviewed and verified any AI-generated content. The order/rule does not specify exactly how litigants must verify the AI-generated content.

disclosure_except_assisted_research.label: Permit with disclosure/certification for non-Westlaw, Lexis, etc.
disclosure_except_assisted_research.description: The order/rule permits litigants to use AI and requires them to disclose and certify their AI usage. However, the disclosure and certification requirement does not apply to the use of legal tools such as Westlaw or Lexis.

permitted_with_caution.label: Permit with general accuracy obligations
permitted_with_caution.description: The order/rule permits litigants to use any AI. It may remind them of their obligations under FRCP Rule 11 or to verify the accuracy of any AI-generated content, but it does not require that litigants explicitly disclose and certify AI usage.

no_explicit_rule.label: No explicit AI rule
no_explicit_rule.description: Court or judge has standing orders or local rules but none specifically address AI.

permitted.label: Permitted with no qualifications
permitted.description: AI is permitted, with no other conditions or reminders. This category is quite rare.

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Illinois
rules.title: Standing Order for Civil Cases Before Magistrate Judge Fuentes
rules.summary: "The Court refers all counsel to the Illinois Supreme Court's policy on artificial intelligence use, effective January 1, 2025. The Court expects that no counsel will submit hallucinated authorities and that all counsel will follow Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. ... The Court will continue to presume that the preexisting Rule 11 certification is a representation by filers, as living, breathing, thinking human beings, that they themselves have read and analyzed all cited authorities to ensure that such authorities actually exist and that the filings comply with Rule 11(b)(2)." The referenced Illinois Supreme Court policy states: "The use of AI by litigants, attorneys, judges, judicial clerks, research attorneys, and court staff providing similar support may be expected, should not be discouraged, and is authorized, provided it complies with legal and ethical standards. Disclosure of AI use should not be required in a pleading."
rules.verbatim_key_language: The Court refers all counsel to the Illinois Supreme Court's policy on artificial intelligence use, effective January 1, 2025. The Court expects that no counsel will submit hallucinated authorities and that all counsel will follow Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure.
rules.provenance: manually verified 2026-04-17; adopts Illinois Supreme Court AI policy (https://ilcourtsaudio.blob.core.windows.net/antilles-resources/resources/e43964ab-8874-4b7a-be4e-63af019cb6f7/Illinois%20Supreme%20Court%20AI%20Policy.pdf) which authorizes AI use and states disclosure should not be required

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania
rules.title: Standing Order Re: Use of Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: Requires counsel to disclose any use of AI in preparing a filing and to certify that the accuracy of all citations and legal arguments has been verified.
rules.provenance: manually verified 2026-04-17; Baylson is now a senior judge (not active); standing order PDF confirmed on court website

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Illinois
rules.title: Standing Order Regarding Professional Responsibility and AI
rules.summary: Reminds attorneys of their duties under Federal Rules of Civil Procedure 11(b) and 26(g) without imposing additional disclosure or certification requirements. Takes a hands-off approach emphasizing existing professional responsibility rules.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; verified at judge's page with AI dropdown section

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Western District of North Carolina
rules.title: Standing Order In Re: Use of Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: "[B]riefs or memoranda submitted to the court must have a certification that no artificial intelligence was used during the research to prepare the document except for that in the standard online legal research sources Bloomberg, FastCase, Lexis or Westlaw." Requires attorney or paralegal verification of every statement and citation for accuracy.
rules.verbatim_key_language: briefs or memoranda submitted to the court must have a certification that no artificial intelligence was used during the research to prepare the document except for that in the standard online legal research sources
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; verified from court PDF

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Ohio
rules.title: Standing Order on the Use of Generative AI
rules.summary: Prohibits use of AI in the preparation of any filing submitted to the Court, with exception for information gathered from legal search engines such as Westlaw or LexisNexis, or Internet search engines such as Google or Bing. Violations may result in sanctions including striking pleadings, economic sanctions, contempt, or dismissal.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; verified from court PDF

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania
rules.title: Standing Order Re: Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: "[C]ounsel (or a party representing himself or herself) [must] disclose any use of generative Artificial Intelligence ('AI') in the preparation of any complaint, answer, motion, brief, or other paper filed with the Court... [and] certify that each and every citation to the law or the record in the filing has been verified as authentic and accurate."
rules.verbatim_key_language: counsel (or a party representing himself or herself) [must] disclose any use of generative Artificial Intelligence ('AI') in the preparation of any complaint, answer, motion, brief, or other paper filed with the Court
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; verified from court PDF

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Texas
rules.title: General Order 2025-04: Use of Generative AI in Court Filings
rules.summary: Requires attorneys to exercise independent legal judgment and thoroughly review any filing prepared with the assistance of generative AI before submission. Emphasizes that attorneys have a non-delegable responsibility to verify all content in their filings, regardless of whether generative AI was used in preparation.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; verified from court PDF

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of Texas
rules.title: Local Rule CV-11(g): Generative AI Verification Requirement (Amended)
rules.summary: Amended rule requiring all litigants (not just pro se) to review and verify the accuracy and quality of information produced by AI technology that is used in a filing. Expands 2023 rule that only applied to pro se litigants after the court observed increasing numbers of filings that improperly utilized generative AI.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; verified from General Order 25-07

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Texas
rules.title: Local Civil Rule 7.2(f): Disclosure of Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: "A brief prepared using generative artificial intelligence must disclose this fact on the first page under the heading 'Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence.' ... A party who files a brief that does not contain the disclosure required by subsection (f)(1) of this rule certifies that no part of the brief was prepared using generative artificial intelligence."
rules.verbatim_key_language: A brief prepared using generative artificial intelligence must disclose this fact on the first page under the heading 'Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence.'
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; verified from CIVRULES.pdf (effective Sept. 2, 2025 version); supersedes Judge Starr's individual standing order

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California
rules.title: Civil Standing Order (Updated 2025)
rules.summary: "Documents created with [generative AI] must be identified in the title, caption, in a table, or by separate notice... AI-hallucinated citations or fictitious legal citations are prohibited."
rules.verbatim_key_language: Documents created with [generative AI] must be identified in the title, caption, in a table, or by separate notice
rules.provenance: user-verified URLs 2026-04-18; quote from standing order PDF

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Illinois
rules.title: Standing Order Prohibiting AI Use in Drafting
rules.summary: Parties may not use Artificial Intelligence to draft their memoranda or as authority to support their motions. Complete prohibition on AI use in filings.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; verified from court website searches

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Central District of California
rules.title: Standing Order for Civil Cases - AI Use Permitted
rules.summary: AI use by litigants, attorneys, judges, and court staff "may be expected, should not be discouraged, and is authorized provided it complies with legal and ethical standards." Disclosure of AI use is not required in a pleading. Takes permissive approach unlike most federal courts.
rules.verbatim_key_language: may be expected, should not be discouraged, and is authorized provided it complies with legal and ethical standards
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; unique permissive approach

rules.court: Florida Sixth Judicial Circuit Court, Section 22, Family Court Division
rules.title: Standing Order on AI Use in Family Court Filings
rules.summary: "I certify that I have personally reviewed this filing or submission, verified the accuracy of all legal authorities and factual assertions through traditional methods, and conducted a reasonable inquiry into the truth and accuracy of all statements herein." Mandatory certification required on every filing, immediately above the signature line.
rules.verbatim_key_language: verified the accuracy of all legal authorities and factual assertions through traditional methods
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; notable for strong prohibition language

rules.court: California Judicial Council (Statewide)
rules.title: Rule 10.430: Generative Artificial Intelligence Use Policies
rules.summary: "[R]equires disclosure of the use of or reliance on generative AI if the final version of a written, visual, or audio work provided to the public consists entirely of generative AI outputs... [and] prohibit[s] entry of confidential or nonpublic information into public AI systems." First statewide court system framework in the nation. Applies to all California state courts.
rules.verbatim_key_language: requires disclosure of the use of or reliance on generative AI if the final version of a written, visual, or audio work provided to the public consists entirely of generative AI outputs
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; landmark statewide policy, first in U.S.

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado
rules.title: Standing Order Regarding Artificial Intelligence Certification
rules.summary: Requires disclosure when AI is used and certification that all content has been verified for accuracy. Judge Wang has actively enforced this order, issuing an Order to Show Cause in *Coomer v. Lindell* after identifying nearly thirty defective citations in a brief submitted by attorneys who admitted to using generative AI without cite-checking.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; notable for active enforcement through sanctions

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the District of Arizona
rules.title: Standing Order on GenAI Use
rules.summary: Requires attorneys and pro se litigants who use AI to file a separate "Notice of Use of Artificial Intelligence" document that identifies the AI program used, specifies page and line numbers of AI-drafted content, and certifies that AI-generated language was verified for accuracy by a human being.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; mentioned in legal press coverage; primary source not directly accessible

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Western District of Washington
rules.title: Order to Show Cause Re AI Misuse (Enforcement Order)
rules.summary: Judge Cartwright ordered attorney Jocelyn C. Stewart to show cause why she should not face sanctions for AI use that led to submitting "dozens of inaccurate factual and legal citations across at least five different filings." While not a preemptive standing order, this enforcement action signals the court's expectations for AI verification.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; enforcement order rather than preemptive standing order, but signals judicial expectations

rules.court: U.S. Court of International Trade
rules.title: Order on Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: "[A]ny submission containing text drafted with the assistance of a generative artificial intelligence program... must be accompanied by: (1) A disclosure notice that identifies the program used and the specific portion of text that have been so drafted; (2) A certification that the use of such program has not resulted in the disclosure of any confidential or business proprietary information to any unauthorized party."
rules.verbatim_key_language: any submission containing text drafted with the assistance of a generative artificial intelligence program... must be accompanied by: (1) A disclosure notice that identifies the program used and the specific portion of text that have been so drafted; (2) A certification that the use of such program has not resulted in the disclosure of any confidential or business proprietary information
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; second CIT judge order following Vaden

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the District of Hawaii
rules.title: Disclosure and Certification Requirements - Generative Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: Requires disclosure if generative AI is utilized in the preparation of any documents filed with the Court. The guidelines are consistent with Rule 11(b) of the Federal Rules and require certain disclosure and certification requirements when AI is used.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; mirrors Starr-style prototype AI standing order

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the District of New Jersey
rules.title: General Pretrial and Trial Procedures - Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: "The use of any generative AI (such as OpenAI's ChatGPT or Google's Bard) in court filings requires a mandatory disclosure/certification that: (1) identifies the GAI program; (2) identifies the portion of the filing drafted by GAI; and (3) certifies that the GAI work product was diligently reviewed by a human being for accuracy and applicability." Failure to comply may result in sanctions.
rules.verbatim_key_language: The use of any generative AI (such as OpenAI's ChatGPT or Google's Bard) in court filings requires a mandatory disclosure/certification that: (1) identifies the GAI program; (2) identifies the portion of the filing drafted by GAI; and (3) certifies that the GAI work product was diligently reviewed by a human being for accuracy and applicability
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; proactive amendment to pretrial procedures in late 2023

rules.court: U.S. Bankruptcy Court for the Southern District of California
rules.title: General Order 210: Filings Using Generative Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: Court-wide general order governing the use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) for all pleadings, motions, and papers filed in Court proceedings. Establishes requirements and guidelines for attorneys and parties who use generative AI tools when preparing court filings in bankruptcy proceedings.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; issued November 2025, effective January 1, 2026

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the District of Nebraska
rules.title: NECivR 7.0.1: Certificate Regarding Generative Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: District-wide local rule requiring a certificate stating either that no generative AI was used, or that a human verified all AI-generated text including citations. Nebraska is one of only two federal districts (along with Hawaii) with a district-wide AI disclosure requirement.
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; one of two federal districts with district-wide requirement

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the District of Montana
rules.title: Pro Hac Vice Admission Order - AI Prohibition
rules.summary: "Use of artificial intelligence automated drafting programs, such as Chat GPT, is prohibited." This prohibition is limited to counsel appearing pro hac vice (out-of-state attorneys seeking temporary admission to practice in Montana federal court), making it narrower in scope than most AI standing orders.
rules.verbatim_key_language: Use of artificial intelligence automated drafting programs, such as Chat GPT, is prohibited
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; one of earliest AI orders, limited to pro hac vice counsel

rules.court: U.S. District Court for the Western District of Oklahoma
rules.title: Disclosure and Certification Requirements - Generative Artificial Intelligence
rules.summary: Requires parties that used generative AI to draft or prepare a court filing to disclose "that [G]AI was used and the specific [G]AI tool that was used." Also requires attorneys and unrepresented parties to "certify in the document that the person has checked the accuracy of any portion of the document drafted by [G]AI, including all citations and legal authority."
rules.provenance: agent discovery 2026-04; mirrors Starr prototype, adopted by Judge Robertson in E.D. Okla.
